title: Data Types & Operations
description: An overview of Python's built-in data types: integers, floats, strings, booleans, and None — plus operators, type conversion, and common patterns.

sections.title: Numeric Types (int, float)
sections.description: Python supports integers with arbitrary precision and double-precision floating-point numbers. It automatically promotes integers to avoid overflow.
sections.codeBlock:
# Integers (arbitrary precision)
age = 30
print(age)           # 30
hex_val = 0xFF
print(hex_val)       # 255
binary = 0b1010
print(binary)        # 10
large_int = 10 ** 100
print(large_int)     # works out-of-the-box

# Floating-point (IEEE 754 double)
pi = 3.14159
print(pi)            # 3.14159
sci_notation = 1.5e-4
print(sci_notation)  # 0.00015

# True division vs floor division
result_true = 10 / 3
print(result_true)   # 3.3333333333333335 (always returns float)
result_floor = 10 // 3
print(result_floor)  # 3 (rounds toward negative infinity)

sections.title: Text Sequence Type (str)
sections.description: Strings are immutable sequences of Unicode characters. They can be declared with single, double, or triple quotes (for multi-line strings).
sections.codeBlock:
# Single/Double quoted strings
name = "Alice"
print(name)          # Alice
char = 'A'
print(char)          # A

# Multi-line strings
multi = """This is a\nmulti-line string"""
print(multi)

# Escaping and Raw Strings
escaped = "tab:\ttab"
print(escaped)       # interprets \t as a tab
raw = r"C:\path\to\file"
print(raw)           # treats backslashes as literals

sections.title: Boolean and None Types
sections.description: Booleans represent truth values and are a subclass of integer. None represents the absence of a value (similar to null in other languages).
sections.codeBlock:
# Booleans (True / False)
is_active = True
print(is_active)     # True
is_done = False
print(is_done)       # False

# Booleans behave as integers (1 and 0)
bool_sum = True + True + False
print(bool_sum)      # 2

# NoneType
result = None
print(result)        # None

# Identity checks (use 'is' for None, not '==')
is_none = result is None
print(is_none)       # True

sections.title: Type Conversion & Casting
sections.description: Casting functions let you convert values between types explicitly. Python also uses boolean evaluation to check truthiness of objects.
sections.codeBlock:
# Explicit Type Conversion
num_from_str = int("42")
print(num_from_str)  # 42
float_from_str = float("3.14")
print(float_from_str) # 3.14
str_from_num = str(100)
print(str_from_num)  # "100"

# Boolean Conversion (Truthiness)
print(bool(1))       # True
print(bool(0))       # False
print(bool(""))      # False (empty string is falsy)
print(bool([]))      # False (empty list is falsy)
print(bool([1]))     # True

sections.title: Operators & Expressions
sections.description: Operators enable math, comparison, logical reasoning, and membership checks. Logic operations support short-circuit evaluation.
sections.codeBlock:
# Arithmetic Operators
add = 10 + 3
print(add)           # 13
sub = 10 - 3
print(sub)           # 7
mul = 10 * 3
print(mul)           # 30
mod = 10 % 3
print(mod)           # 1
exp = 10 ** 3
print(exp)           # 1000

# Comparison Operators
equal = 10 == 3
print(equal)         # False
not_equal = 10 != 3
print(not_equal)     # True
greater = 10 > 3
print(greater)       # True

# Logical Operators (Short-circuiting)
logical_and = True and False
print(logical_and)   # False
logical_or = True or False
print(logical_or)    # True
logical_not = not True
print(logical_not)   # False

# Membership & Identity Operators
membership_in = "ice" in "Alice"
print(membership_in) # True
membership_not = "x" not in "hello"
print(membership_not) # True
result = None
identity_is = result is not None
print(identity_is)   # False

details:
Python is dynamically typed: variables carry type information at runtime, and the same name can refer to different types over its lifetime. The type() function returns the type of any object; isinstance(obj, type) checks inheritance. All types in Python are objects — even int, float, and bool are instances of classes that define their behavior via operator overloading (dunder methods like __add__, __eq__).

Integers (int) have arbitrary precision, bounded only by available memory. Python automatically promotes results to int or float as needed — division with / always returns a float. The // operator performs floor division, rounding toward negative infinity (not truncation). Floats (float) are IEEE 754 double-precision; for decimal arithmetic, use the decimal module.

Strings (str) are immutable sequences of Unicode code points. Python 3 strings are Unicode by default — no separate byte-string type. Raw strings (r"...") disable escape sequence processing, useful for regex patterns and file paths. For comprehensive string method documentation, see the official str documentation.

Booleans (bool) are a subclass of int — True == 1 and False == 0 evaluate to True, enabling compact patterns like sum([True, False, True]) # 2. Short-circuit evaluation means and and or stop evaluating as soon as the result is determined, returning the last-evaluated operand.

Type conversion follows consistent rules. Many types have boolean truthiness: 0, 0.0, "", [], {}, None are False; everything else is True. The is operator compares object identity (memory address), not value — use == for value equality and is only for singleton checks like x is None.

For a complete reference, see Python operator precedence and the Built-in Types documentation.
